meta.construct_target: Adaptive message structuring under audience constraints — selecting the right content, format, medium, and framing for a specific audience and goal

part_a_construct_diagnosis.summary: 14 of 16 Communication items are STRUCTURALLY FLAWED or BORDERLINE. They measure communication style preference (how you like to communicate) rather than adaptive communication under audience constraints. Zero items encode a real audience mismatch or communication trade-off. Most items ask 'how do you typically communicate?' rather than 'how do you adapt communication when the audience requires something different from your default?'

items.stem: In a team meeting, how do you typically contribute?
options: I listen carefully and ask clarifying questions
I share ideas and build on others' suggestions
I take notes and summarize key points
I focus on actionable next steps
specific_problems: 'Listen carefully' is social desirability magnet — signals humility
'Share ideas and build on others' is agreeableness/collaboration — not communication skill
'Take notes and summarize' is a task role, not a communication strategy
'Focus on actionable next steps' is Drive/action-orientation, not communication
Stem says 'typically' — measures habit, not adaptive skill

items.stem: How do you handle conflicts or disagreements at work?
options: Find common ground and shared solutions
Present facts and data to support my position
specific_problems: 'Find common ground' is social desirability magnet — dominance risk 31%
'Facilitate discussion' signals diplomatic maturity — sophistication gradient
'Present facts and data' cross-loads with CP (structured argumentation)
Some legitimate conflict-navigation content but no audience-specific adaptation

items.stem: How do you adapt your communication style to different audiences?
options: Study the background and needs of the audience
Use examples and analogies relevant to their context
Modify the level of technical detail
Ask for feedback to ensure I'm being clear
missing: No trade-off between competing communication strategies
All options are 'good practice' — no tension
specific_problems: Stem directly asks about adaptation but options are all equally valid best practices
'Study the background' is Knowledge behavior (research), not communication
'Ask for feedback' is social desirability magnet — signals humility
No cognitive tension — a competent person would do ALL of these
Measures 'do you know what good communication looks like?' not 'can you do it under pressure?'

items.stem: How do you present complex results to non-technical stakeholders?
options: Focus on business results, not technical details
Tell a story that connects data to concrete impacts
Prepare executive summaries with key points
specific_problems: 'Tell a story' is sophistication/charisma gradient — sounds most impressive
'Use charts' is tool preference, not communication strategy
Good scenario setup (non-technical stakeholders) but options are all 'best practices'
No tension between competing communication needs (e.g., accuracy vs simplicity, speed vs depth)

items.stem: In an exec meeting with conflicting opinions, you typically:
options: Synthesize and reframe the core issue
Ask clarifying questions to align context
De-escalate and redirect to shared goals
specific_problems: 'Synthesize and reframe' sounds most intellectually sophisticated — dominance risk 30%
'De-escalate and redirect' is agreeableness/conflict-avoidance — personality trait
Better than science_tech q2 because it has a scenario (conflicting opinions) but still measures preference
Stem says 'typically' — habit not adaptive skill

items.stem: When a team resists a strategic change, you:
options: Tell a compelling story with evidence
missing: No specific constraint on HOW you can communicate
specific_problems: 'Map stakeholders and tailor messages' is the textbook-correct answer — signals training/sophistication
'Tell a compelling story' is charisma/narrative skill — not structural adaptation
'Create forums' cross-loads with Drive (creating opportunities for engagement)
Good scenario but options range from 'obviously right' to 'obviously less sophisticated'
Pure format preference — 'how do you like to communicate?'
'Tell a narrative with outcomes' is sophistication gradient
'Offer clear calls to action' is Drive/action-orientation, not communication
No tension between competing communication needs
A skilled communicator would use ALL of these depending on context

options: Tell a story tied to impact
specific_problems: Identical construct as q12 — both ask 'how do you present things?'
'Focus on trade-offs and choices' cross-loads with CP (analytical framing)
'Tell a story' appears AGAIN — repeated sophistication signal
No cognitive tension whatsoever

items.stem: Presenting a concept to non-designers, you:
specific_problems: 'Tell the story behind choices' is the sophistication/charisma magnet — again
'Relate benefits to business goals' is good audience adaptation but sounds like 'best practice'
'Invite questions' is social desirability — signals openness
Good scenario (non-designers) but no tension in options
'Paraphrase to ensure understanding' is textbook active listening — social desirability magnet (dominance 29%)
'Propose a revision plan' is Drive (action-orientation), not communication
Measures 'how emotionally mature are you when criticized?' not 'how do you adapt communication'
Some listening calibration content but no cognitive tension

items.stem: When teams misunderstand the brief, you:
missing: No specific constraint on HOW you can clarify
specific_problems: 'Reframe outcomes and success' is reframing — cross-loads with Creativity
'Translate abstract goals to criteria' is CP (structured decomposition)
Good scenario (misunderstanding) but options are all equally valid approaches
'Co-write a clarified brief' is collaborative — could be Communication or just good practice
Pure format preference — identical to BL q12 and BL q17
'Show options with pros/cons' cross-loads with CP
'Simple narrative arcs' is sophistication gradient
Zero cognitive tension

missing: No constraint on HOW you respond
specific_problems: 'Listen and reflect needs' is social desirability magnet — signals empathy (dominance 30%)
'Escalate appropriately' is process adherence, not communication skill
'Offer options and next steps' is Drive/action-orientation
Good scenario (difficult customer) but options are all 'correct' customer service behaviors
'Explain the why and impact' is the textbook correct answer — social desirability dominant (33%)
'Pair people for peer support' is a training strategy, not communication
'Offer a Q&A channel' is medium selection but without constraint it's just preference
No cognitive tension — all options are things a good manager would do

items.stem: When teams hand off work, you:
specific_problems: 'Define clear acceptance criteria' is CP (structuring requirements)
'Create shared dashboards' is tooling preference, not communication
This is a process design question, not a communication question
Zero connection to adaptive message structuring

missing: No constraint on which medium to use
specific_problems: Pure medium/channel selection without any constraint or trade-off
A good ops manager would select based on context — but the question doesn't give context
Interchangeable with a 'what tools do you prefer?' survey
Zero cognitive tension

part_b_social_desirability_gradient.summary: 11 of 16 items have at least one option with predicted selection ≥28%, and 5 items have an option at or above 30%. The dominant pattern is 'empathetic listening' and 'storytelling' options attracting disproportionate selection due to social desirability.

gradient_summary.most_repeated_magnet: 'Tell a story / narrative' appears in 6 of 16 items as an option — creating a consistent sophistication signal across the entire pillar

part_c_cross_pillar_bleed.summary: Communication items show significant cross-pillar contamination. 4 items exceed the 30% confusion risk threshold, and 7 more are in the 25-30% range.

part_d_adversarial_profiles.summary: The current Communication items fail to discriminate between genuine adaptive communicators and superficial communication styles. The charismatic-but-shallow and conflict-avoidant archetypes both score HIGH — indicating the items measure social desirability and personality traits rather than communication skill.

archetypes.label: Charismatic but shallow communicator
archetypes.description: Articulate, engaging, good storytelling. But doesn't adapt message to audience needs — uses the same charming approach regardless of context.

science_tech.q2: B ('share ideas and build') — charming contributor

archetypes.problem: SCORES HIGH — charming default responses align with social desirability gradients. The items reward narrative skill and agreeableness, which is exactly what this archetype has.

archetypes.label: Introverted but precise communicator
archetypes.description: Quiet, deliberate. Adapts message carefully to audience, selects medium thoughtfully, structures information for clarity. Strong adaptive communicator.

science_tech.q2: C ('take notes and summarize') — quiet contributor
science_tech.q7: B ('present facts and data') — precise approach
science_tech.q12: C ('modify technical detail level') — genuine adaptation

archetypes.problem: SCORES LOW — precise, adaptive communication is penalized because the items reward extroverted/narrative options. This archetype's genuine communication skill is invisible to the current instrument.

archetypes.label: Dominant debater
archetypes.description: Forceful, articulate, wins arguments. Doesn't listen or adapt — imposes their framing on every audience.
archetypes.problem: SCORES MEDIUM-HIGH — dominant debaters select 'sophisticated' options that align with the desirability gradient. The items don't distinguish between persuasion (imposing your frame) and adaptation (changing your frame to match the audience).

archetypes.label: Conflict-avoidant people-pleaser
archetypes.description: Agreeable, diplomatic, avoids confrontation. Says what people want to hear rather than what they need to hear. Poor at delivering difficult messages.

science_tech.q17: B ('focus on business results') — safe choice

archetypes.problem: SCORES HIGH — the empathy/listening/diplomacy social desirability magnets perfectly match this archetype's defaults. The items cannot distinguish between genuine empathy and conflict avoidance.

archetypes.label: Highly analytical but blunt candidate
archetypes.description: Clear, direct, data-driven communication. Doesn't adjust for audience emotional state or context. Says exactly what they mean without softening.

service_ops.q2: B ('clarify policies in plain language') — direct

archetypes.problem: SCORES LOW — bluntness is penalized but not for the right reason. This archetype should score low on Communication because they don't adapt, but the items penalize them because they don't select the 'empathetic/narrative' options, not because they fail to adapt.

discrimination_summary.root_cause: Items measure communication STYLE (narrative, empathetic, diplomatic) not communication SKILL (adaptive structuring under audience constraints)

rationale: 7/16 items are STRUCTURALLY FLAWED (measure preferences, not skills)
6/16 items have social desirability dominant options (≥30%)
Charismatic-shallow (72nd) and conflict-avoidant (68th) both score HIGH — proving items measure personality, not communication skill
Introverted-precise communicator (38th) scores LOW — proving items penalize introverted communication styles regardless of adaptive skill
'Tell a story / narrative' appears in 6/16 items as an option — creating a systematic sophistication bias
The construct measured is 'communication style identity' not 'adaptive message structuring under audience constraints'
redesign_requirements: Every question must encode a SPECIFIC audience mismatch or communication constraint
Options must represent competing communication STRATEGIES, not format preferences
No option may signal empathy, diplomacy, or narrative skill as inherently superior
Charismatic-shallow and conflict-avoidant archetypes must score LOW
